common_name: Carter's Sunburst Camellia (Striped Pale-Pink Exhibition-Form Japonica Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Carter's Sunburst is an outstanding exhibition camellia and one of the most distinctive for its striped, spotted, and suffused pink-on-pale-pink flower coloring that sets it apart from solid-color camellias.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Carter's Sunburst is one of the most immediately recognizable Camellia japonica cultivars — producing large, semi-double to peony-form flowers in pale pink to soft blush with overlaid deep pink stripes, streaks, and blotches distributed across the petals in patterns that are never exactly the same flower-to-flower. The striped and spotted flower coloring is distinctive enough that Carter's Sunburst is among the most frequently identified camellias by home gardeners and garden visitors — the unpredictable pink-on-pale-pink pattern creates visual complexity that solid-color camellias cannot match. The flowers are large (typically 4–5 inches across), combining good exhibition size with the informal charm of variegated coloring. Carter's Sunburst is a mid-season bloomer (typically February–March in zone 9), flowering after the earliest camellias and before the latest, filling a useful mid-winter bloom window. The plant grows as a medium-to-large, dense, upright evergreen shrub with the characteristically glossy, dark-green camellia foliage that provides attractive year-round structure. In zone 9, Carter's Sunburst is a reliable perennial landscape shrub requiring primarily acidic soil, consistent moisture, and protection from afternoon sun that can bleach the pale-pink flower color. An outstanding specimen plant near garden entrances where the distinctive striped flowers can be seen closely.

common_name: Fragrant Pink Camellia (Pioneer Sweet-Scented Small-Flower Hybrid Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Fragrant Pink was one of the first fragrant camellias widely available in the nursery trade and established the concept of fragrant camellia hybrids before the later High Fragrance and other cultivars expanded the group.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; Fragrant Pink is a hybrid Camellia bred to introduce the sweet floral fragrance of Camellia lutchuensis into cultivars with the refined flower form of Camellia japonica — a cross that transformed the possibilities of camellia breeding by combining beauty and scent that had previously been impossible in a single plant. The flowers of Fragrant Pink are small to medium-sized, peony-form to double, in clear soft rose-pink — the individual flower size is smaller than most japonica cultivars, a characteristic inherited from the C. lutchuensis parent, but the sweet, true floral fragrance detectable from several feet away compensates fully for the smaller bloom size. The scent is genuine and pleasant — not a faint hint but a real, detectable, jasmine-like sweetness that makes this camellia suitable for planting near outdoor living areas. Fragrant Pink typically blooms in late winter (January–March in zone 9), filling the period when the garden has few fragrant flowering plants. The plant grows as a compact to medium evergreen shrub — somewhat smaller than most C. japonica cultivars — with a densely branched, naturally rounded habit. In zone 9, it is a reliable landscape perennial performing best with morning sun and afternoon shade. The fragrant flower characteristic and compact habit make Fragrant Pink an ideal camellia for planting near patios, garden seats, and paths where the sweet winter fragrance enhances the garden experience.

common_name: White Adriatic Fig (Classic Italian Green-Skinned Honey-Flavored Jam Fig)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Adriatic is a traditional Italian fig variety commercially cultivated in California and historically along the Gulf Coast — the thin skin and high sugar content that make it exceptional for fresh eating and jam also make it less suitable for humid climates where skin cracking and splitting can be a problem in rainy weather at ripening time.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Adriatic is a classic Italian fig cultivar grown commercially in California (it is the primary variety used for dried figs and fig paste in California production) and traditionally cultivated in Gulf South kitchen gardens for its exceptional fresh-eating quality. The medium-to-large fruits have green-to-yellow-green skin at full ripeness with amber to light strawberry-rose interior flesh of unusually high sweetness and a clean, honey-like flavor that ranks among the most highly regarded of all common fig varieties for fresh use. The flavor of fully ripe White Adriatic fresh from the tree — warm, sweet, delicate — is the benchmark against which many other figs are judged. The thin skin is part of what makes the fresh flavor exceptional; it also means ripe fruits are delicate and should be harvested promptly before rain or excess humidity causes splitting. White Adriatic is a vigorous, productive fig tree that typically begins bearing in its second year from planting and reaches full production by year 3–5. In Gulf South zone 9, the main crop ripens in late July through September; a breba (early) crop may develop on previous year's wood in June in years when the wood survives winter undamaged. Self-fertile, requiring no pollinators. Best eaten fresh within a day or two of harvest; or made into fig preserves and jam where the delicate honey flavor is retained.

common_name: Nottingham Medlar (Classic Bletted European Medlar Tree for Unusual Jelly and Wine)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nottingham is the most widely grown and most readily available medlar cultivar in commerce — selected for more open, easier-to-pick fruit structure than the species and reliable flavor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nottingham Medlar is the best and most widely grown cultivar of one of the most unusual and historically significant fruits in the European garden tradition — the medlar (Mespilus germanica) is a small deciduous tree that produces brown, apple-like fruits with an open calyx end that must undergo 'bletting' (controlled softening through frost damage and fermentation) before becoming edible. Freshly picked medlar fruits are hard, astringent, and unpleasant; after bletting — either on the tree after frosts or stored in a cool location for several weeks — they transform into soft, brown, date-like paste with a rich, wine-like flavor combining apple, date, and brown sugar notes that is quite unlike any other fruit. Bletted medlar flesh is used for medlar jelly (a beautiful, rose-colored preserve with complex flavor), medlar wine, and eaten fresh as a confection. The tree itself is ornamentally beautiful: a small, naturally gnarled and picturesque deciduous tree (10–15 feet) with large white flowers in spring, handsome broad leaves that color well in autumn, and the unusual open-calyx brown fruits hanging from the branches through fall and into winter. The late-season beauty and ancient cultural history — medlar appears frequently in medieval and Renaissance English literature and was a common kitchen-garden tree for centuries — give this fruit exceptional interest for historically minded gardeners. Zone 9 is at the warm edge of its range.

common_name: Fresh Look Yellow Celosia (AAS-Winning Bright Yellow Long-Season Cockscomb)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after the last frost, or direct sow after soil is consistently warm (75°F+); Fresh Look Yellow maintains its vibrant golden-yellow color throughout the season without fading, a trait that earned it AAS recognition over earlier yellow celosia selections that tended to fade to cream in heat.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fresh Look Yellow is an All-America Selections winner in the cockscomb (cristata) type of Celosia argentea — producing rounded, brain-like, velvety crested flower heads in clear, vivid golden yellow that maintains its color throughout the long season without fading to cream or pale yellow as earlier yellow celosia varieties tended to do in Gulf South summer heat. The color consistency was the key improvement that earned the AAS award — a true, saturated golden-yellow that holds its intensity from first bloom through fall frost, providing a reliable color anchor in summer and fall mixed borders. The crested, velvety flower heads have a unique texture — velvet-soft, deeply convoluted, with a rich tactile quality that makes them as interesting to touch as to see — and the heads last for months on the plant without deadheading. Fresh Look Yellow is an outstanding cut flower and dried flower plant: the vivid yellow crested heads dry naturally on the stem without significant color loss, making them valuable for dried arrangements that last for months after harvest. Excellent in mixed summer combination plantings with deep blue salvias, orange lantana, and bronze foliage plants. In Gulf South zone 9, celosia can often be direct-seeded in warm soil for a late-summer crop when transplant-grown plants have passed peak.
